one_line_takeaway: R2A shows that cost-aware black-box LLM routers can be reliably tricked into picking expensive models by appending an optimized universal suffix learned from only ~120 observed routing decisions (~$0.98), inflating serving cost roughly 2.7-2.9x, reframing routing as a security-critical boundary.
key_finding: R2A achieves state-of-the-art ASR across all six target routers and both in- and out-of-distribution query distributions, beating clean queries and all baselines. Per-router average ASR (with lift over clean): RouteLLM-Bert 0.89 (+0.49 over clean 0.40), GraphRouter 0.87 (+0.23), P2L 0.74 (+0.07), RouterDC 0.94 (+0.22), RouteLLM-MF 0.95 (+0.39), and the real-world black-box OpenRouter 0.74 (+0.47 over clean 0.27). The attack is cheap and sample-efficient: collecting the 120 surrogate-training queries costs only about $0.98, and ASR saturates around 120 queries (e.g., RouteLLM-Bert rises from 0.58 to 0.87 as budget grows 80->120). It measurably raises victim cost: ~2.7x cost-per-million-tokens on MMLU and ~2.9x on RouterArena versus clean. Against the closed GPT-5 Auto router, transferred suffixes shift responses toward 'Thinking'-style strong-model outputs and win the LLM-judge comparison 64% vs 36% overall (and on comprehensiveness/empowerment 64%/64%, diversity 72%).
core_contribution: The paper introduces R2A (Route to Rome Attack), the first fully black-box adversarial-suffix attack that manipulates cost-aware LLM routers into consistently dispatching queries to expensive high-capability models. Its own two technical contributions are: (1) a hybrid ensemble surrogate router that combines several pre-trained open-source routers with a LoRA-style low-rank trainable lightweight router, jointly learning ensemble weights and lightweight-router parameters under a strict query budget so it can mimic an unknown target router observed only through its final routing decisions; and (2) a tailored discrete suffix-optimization algorithm (a GCG variant) that aggregates token gradients across the heterogeneous ensemble members via per-router min-max gradient normalization to prevent any single encoder from dominating. The attacker appends one universal, target-specific suffix to the query to force weak-model queries into the strong-model set. Note the prior methods Rerouting (Shafran et al. 2025) and LifeCycle (Lin et al. 2025b) are cited baselines / motivation, NOT this paper's contribution; R2A's distinction is optimizing suffixes with only observed routing decisions (no gradients/parameters).
threat_model: Realistic commercial black-box setting. Attacker's goal: given a query q that the target router Rt would send to a cheap/weak model (Rt(q) in Mweak), append a suffix so Rt(q+s) selects an expensive/strong model (Mstrong). Strong vs weak partition is drawn from public leaderboards (lmarena.ai). Attacker capability: can only append an adversarial suffix of at most Delta tokens to the query (no other modification). Attacker knowledge: black-box; can observe the router's candidate model pool and its final selected-model decision (assumed exposed for billing transparency) but NOT internal logits, parameters, or gradients. Each probe of the target costs money, so the attacker is capped at Q=120 queries to build the surrogate. Impact: inflated inference cost / economic denial-of-sustainability against the router operator (and potentially latency), rather than content-safety compromise.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) the attack only steers toward stronger/more-expensive models and does not investigate targeting a specific model for other motives such as latency or safety; (2) it assumes the attacker can see the router's candidate model list and the identity of the model selected per query, which may not hold in all deployments. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: a separate suffix must be trained per target router (not one universal suffix across routers); GPT-5 evaluation is indirect (LLM-judge win rate and BoW fingerprinting proxies) because true routing decisions are unobservable, so ASR cannot be computed there; robustness is tested against only one defense (whitespace insertion), which produces small ASR drops; and gains on some routers (P2L, +0.07 avg) are marginal, so effectiveness is uneven across router architectures.

quantitative_results: R2A average ASR per target router: RouteLLM-Bert 0.89 (clean 0.40), GraphRouter 0.87 (clean 0.64), P2L 0.74 (clean 0.67), RouterDC 0.94 (clean 0.72), RouteLLM-MF 0.95 (clean 0.56), OpenRouter 0.74 (clean 0.27)
Largest per-cell lifts: OpenRouter MMLU 0.12->0.89 (+0.77), RouteLLM-Bert SimpleQA 0.24->1.00 (+0.76), RouteLLM-MF MMLU 0.38->0.98 (+0.60)
Inference cost increase after attack: ~2.7x per million tokens on MMLU, ~2.9x on RouterArena vs clean
Attacker cost to collect 120 surrogate-training queries: ~$0.98; query budget Q=120
Sample efficiency: RouteLLM-Bert ASR 0.58->0.87 as query budget grows 80->120; performance saturates around 120 queries
GPT-5 Auto LLM-judge win rate (attacked vs clean): Overall 64% vs 36%, Comprehensiveness 64% vs 36%, Diversity 72% vs 28%, Empowerment 64% vs 36%
Ablation: removing gradient normalization drops RouteLLM-MF ASR 0.95->0.49; removing lightweight router drops RouterDC 0.83->0.30
Whitespace defense causes only slight ASR drops, e.g., RouteLLM-Bert ArenaHard 0.84->0.81, GraphRouter ArenaHard 0.83->0.73, RouterDC unchanged at 1.00
baselines_compared: Rerouting (Shafran et al. 2025) - hill-climbing query-independent trigger
LifeCycle (W) (Lin et al. 2025b) - white-box gradient-optimized trigger
LifeCycle (B) (Lin et al. 2025b) - black-box trigger extracted from high-win-rate queries via GPT-4o
Chain-of-Thought / CoT (Kojima et al. 2022) - appends 'Let's think step by step'
